questions.question: Is there a written clinical pharmacology plan aligned with the TPP?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates the existence and quality of a clinical pharmacology plan. For biologics, plan must address biologic-specific elements: immunogenicity impact on PK/PD, TMDD characterisation strategy, receptor occupancy/target coverage approach, and for bispecifics, MABEL-based dose selection and step-up dosing rationale.
questions.rationale: A structured plan guides evidence generation across development. For biologics, the clinical pharmacology plan must address fundamentally different PK mechanisms (TMDD, FcRn), immunogenicity impact on exposure, and for bispecifics, dose-finding complexities (bell-shaped dose-response, MABEL, step-up dosing) that do not exist for small molecules.

questions.question: Is the dose selection strategy clear and justified across development stages?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether dose selection is supported by integrated PK/PD/ER analysis. For biologics, includes target coverage/receptor occupancy-based dosing, TMDD-aware dose selection, and for immune-activating biologics/bispecifics, MABEL-based FIH dose with step-up dosing rationale. Assessment of whether flat vs. weight-based dosing is justified.
questions.rationale: Biologic dose selection differs fundamentally from SM. Key decisions include flat vs. weight-based dosing, target coverage-based dose optimisation, and for bispecifics, MABEL-derived starting doses that may be 1000-fold below the efficacious dose, requiring extensive dose escalation. The flat vs. weight-based dosing decision has significant clinical and commercial implications.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology activities integrated into the overall development plan?
questions.rubric_tests: How clinical pharmacology deliverables are coordinated with clinical, regulatory, and CMC timelines. For biologics, includes coordination of immunogenicity monitoring with PK/PD analyses, comparability PK bridging studies when process changes occur, and integration of ADA data into exposure-response analyses.
questions.rationale: Cross-functional integration is essential. For biologics, the interdependence between CMC (process changes requiring PK bridging), immunogenicity (ADA impacting PK/PD), and clinical pharmacology is tighter than for small molecules. Process changes may require clinical PK comparability studies — a uniquely biologic concern.

questions.question: Are PK profiles adequately characterised in the intended patient population?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK is described using appropriate methods (NCA, population PK, TMDD modeling). For biologics, includes characterisation of target-mediated drug disposition (non-linear clearance), FcRn-mediated recycling, distribution to target tissues, and impact of disease state (tumour burden, target expression levels) on PK. Assessment of whether PK is characterised in patients (not just healthy volunteers — many biologics go directly to patients).
questions.rationale: For biologics, PK is typically characterised in patients rather than healthy volunteers (most mAbs and bispecifics target disease-specific antigens making healthy volunteer studies unethical or uninformative). TMDD creates dose-dependent non-linear PK that must be explicitly characterised. Disease state (e.g., tumour burden affecting target-mediated clearance) directly impacts PK.

questions.question: Has dose-exposure linearity been assessed, including characterisation of non-linear PK from target-mediated drug disposition?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the dose-exposure relationship is characterised, including identification of the dose at which target saturation occurs and PK transitions from non-linear (TMDD-dominated) to linear (FcRn-dominated). For bispecifics, assessment of whether dual-target engagement creates complex dose-exposure relationships.
questions.rationale: Unlike SM dose proportionality (driven by absorption/metabolism saturation), biologic non-linearity is driven by TMDD. Below target saturation, clearance is rapid (target-mediated); above saturation, clearance slows dramatically (FcRn-mediated). Understanding this transition is critical for dose selection and dosing interval optimisation.

questions.question: Are single- and multiple-dose PKs evaluated with appropriate sampling to capture biologic disposition phases?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK sampling captures the full disposition profile. For biologics, includes adequate sampling to characterise the distribution phase, TMDD-driven elimination, FcRn-mediated recycling, and terminal elimination. Assessment of whether sampling duration is sufficient (mAb half-lives are typically 7-21 days, requiring extended sampling).
questions.rationale: Biologic PK sampling requires fundamentally different design than SM: sampling must extend over weeks (not hours), must capture the non-linear to linear transition, and must account for ADA-mediated clearance that can emerge at later timepoints. Inadequate sampling duration is a common design flaw in early biologic PK studies.

questions.question: Are accumulation, steady-state, and time-dependent PK changes characterised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether accumulation and steady-state are characterised. For biologics, includes assessment of whether time-dependent clearance changes occur (e.g., from target depletion reducing TMDD over time, or ADA emergence accelerating clearance), and whether dosing interval is optimised for target coverage maintenance.
questions.rationale: For biologics, time-dependent PK changes are common: target depletion over repeated dosing can reduce TMDD and slow clearance; conversely, ADA emergence can accelerate clearance. These dynamics must be understood to optimise dosing regimen and predict long-term exposure.

questions.question: Are inter-individual and intra-individual PK variabilities quantified and sources identified?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK variability is quantified and attributed to identifiable sources. For biologics, key sources of variability include body weight, disease burden (target expression), ADA status, albumin levels (for FcRn recycling), and for bispecifics, tumour burden affecting target-mediated clearance.
questions.rationale: Biologic PK variability is driven by different factors than SM variability. Body weight is a major covariate (informing flat vs. weight-based dosing decisions), ADA status dramatically affects exposure, and disease burden (target expression) modulates TMDD-driven clearance. Understanding these sources is essential for dosing guidance.

questions.question: Is the impact of anti-drug antibodies (ADA) on PK prospectively assessed and integrated into exposure analyses?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether ADA impact on PK is systematically assessed, including clearing ADA (accelerating elimination), neutralising ADA (blocking target engagement), and sustaining ADA (potentially extending half-life). Assessment of whether PK analyses stratify by ADA status and whether ADA-mediated PK changes are modeled.
questions.rationale: ADA can fundamentally alter biologic PK — clearing antibodies accelerate elimination (sometimes reducing exposure 10-fold or more), while neutralising antibodies block pharmacological activity. For biologics, ADA impact on PK is not a secondary analysis but a primary consideration that must be prospectively planned. Failure to account for ADA in PK interpretation can lead to incorrect dose-exposure conclusions.

questions.question: Are there validated PD biomarkers linked to MOA or clinical endpoints?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PD biomarkers reflect target engagement or biological activity. For biologics, includes receptor occupancy (RO), soluble target suppression, target cell depletion, downstream immune markers (cytokines, immune cell phenotyping), and for bispecifics, T-cell activation and tumour cell killing kinetics.
questions.rationale: Validated PD biomarkers are essential for dose selection. For biologics, PD endpoints are often immune-mediated (target cell depletion, cytokine release, T-cell activation) and require specialised assays. Receptor occupancy is the most direct measure of target engagement for mAbs.

questions.question: Is there an E-R model for efficacy? For safety?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether E-R models exist for both efficacy and safety. For biologics, includes assessment of whether models account for TMDD-driven non-linear exposure, ADA impact on exposure, and for bispecifics, bell-shaped E-R relationships and the distinction between E-R for efficacy vs. E-R for CRS/immune toxicity.
questions.rationale: E-R modelling for biologics must account for non-linear PK, ADA-mediated exposure changes, and for bispecifics, the potential for bell-shaped efficacy curves where higher doses reduce efficacy due to competitive inhibition of trimer formation. E-R for safety (particularly CRS) may follow a different relationship than E-R for efficacy.

questions.question: Has a therapeutic index been established or estimated?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the margin between efficacious and toxic exposures is quantified. For biologics, includes assessment of whether the therapeutic window accounts for target coverage requirements (minimum effective exposure) and immune-mediated toxicity thresholds (maximum tolerated exposure, CRS thresholds for bispecifics).
questions.rationale: For biologics, the therapeutic index is defined by target coverage (requiring minimum exposure for efficacy) and immune-mediated toxicity (limiting maximum exposure). For T-cell engaging bispecifics, this window can be narrow — efficacious doses may be close to CRS-inducing doses, requiring careful dose optimisation and mitigation strategies.

questions.question: Are E-R analyses used to support dose selection, including flat vs. weight-based dosing decisions?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether E-R data support dosing decisions. For biologics, includes use of E-R to justify flat dosing vs. weight-based dosing (a major commercial and clinical decision), dosing interval selection (target trough-based), and dose optimisation across indications.
questions.rationale: The flat vs. weight-based dosing decision is one of the most consequential E-R-driven decisions for biologics — it affects clinical convenience, commercial strategy, and drug product configuration. E-R analyses must determine whether body weight-driven exposure variability is clinically meaningful enough to warrant weight-based dosing or whether flat dosing provides equivalent outcomes.

questions.question: For bispecifics: is the relationship between exposure, target cell engagement, immune activation, and clinical response characterised, including potential bell-shaped dose-response?
questions.rubric_tests: Assessment of whether the multi-dimensional E-R relationship for bispecifics is characterised: exposure vs. target cell killing (efficacy), exposure vs. cytokine release (CRS risk), exposure vs. T-cell activation/exhaustion (sustained efficacy), and whether a bell-shaped dose-response is observed. Evaluation of whether the optimal biological dose (OBD) is identified distinct from the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).
questions.rationale: Bispecific E-R is fundamentally more complex than conventional mAb or SM E-R. T-cell engaging bispecifics can show bell-shaped efficacy curves (excess drug blocks trimer formation), dissociation between efficacy and toxicity E-R, and time-dependent changes in E-R as T-cells exhaust or targets deplete. Identifying the OBD (not just MTD) is a key regulatory and clinical challenge for bispecifics.

questions.question: Is SC bioavailability characterised if SC administration is planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether absolute bioavailability of the SC formulation is determined (relative to IV), including assessment of Tmax, absorption rate, and factors affecting SC absorption (injection volume, concentration, injection site, formulation composition). For biologics, SC bioavailability is typically 50-80% and highly variable — this must be quantified.
questions.rationale: SC administration is increasingly preferred for biologics (patient convenience, self-administration). SC bioavailability for mAbs is inherently variable (50-80%) due to lymphatic uptake, pre-systemic catabolism, and formulation-dependent absorption. Quantifying SC bioavailability and understanding its variability is essential for SC dose selection and IV-to-SC switching strategies.

questions.question: Are manufacturing process changes bridged by clinical PK comparability data?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether process changes during development (scale-up, site transfer, cell culture modifications) are supported by clinical PK bridging data demonstrating comparable exposure. Assessment of whether PK comparability studies are designed with appropriate statistical criteria (equivalence bounds, sample size) and whether analytical comparability data support waiver of clinical bridging for minor changes.
questions.rationale: For biologics, "the process is the product" — manufacturing process changes can alter the molecule (glycosylation, charge variants, aggregation) and consequently its PK. Clinical PK comparability is a regulatory expectation for significant process changes per ICH Q5E. This has no SM parallel (SM formulation bridging is driven by dissolution/absorption, not molecular changes).

questions.question: For IV-to-SC switching programmes: is the SC dose justified with PK/PD modelling to achieve comparable target coverage?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether IV-to-SC dose conversion is supported by PK/PD modeling demonstrating equivalent target coverage (trough concentration, receptor occupancy, AUC) at the proposed SC dose and interval. Assessment of whether the higher Cmax from IV (vs. lower, sustained exposure from SC) affects safety or efficacy differently.
questions.rationale: IV-to-SC switching is a major lifecycle strategy for biologics. The dose conversion is not simple arithmetic — different PK profiles (IV bolus peak vs. SC sustained trough) may produce different PD effects. PK/PD modeling must demonstrate that the SC regimen achieves equivalent target coverage. This is a rapidly growing area with multiple approved mAb SC formulations.

questions.question: Has the potential for immune-mediated drug interactions (cytokine-mediated CYP modulation) been assessed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the biologic's potential to modulate CYP enzyme activity through cytokine-mediated effects has been assessed. For immune-modulating mAbs and bispecifics, elevated cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ) can suppress CYP activity, while anti-cytokine therapies (e.g., anti-IL-6) can restore CYP activity — altering exposure of co-administered SM drugs. Assessment per FDA guidance on therapeutic protein DDIs.
questions.rationale: Biologics do not directly interact with CYP enzymes or transporters, but immune-modulating biologics can indirectly alter SM drug metabolism through cytokine-mediated CYP regulation. This is the primary DDI mechanism for biologics and is explicitly addressed in FDA guidance. Programmes must assess whether their biologic's pharmacology could alter cytokine milieu sufficiently to affect co-administered SM PK.

questions.question: Are pharmacokinetic interactions with co-administered biologics or standard-of-care therapies characterised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK interactions between the biologic and co-administered therapies (other biologics, chemotherapy, immunosuppressants) are characterised. For bispecifics used with checkpoint inhibitors or chemotherapy, assessment of whether combination PK is evaluated and whether dose adjustments are needed.
questions.rationale: Biologics are increasingly used in combination — bispecifics with checkpoint inhibitors, mAbs with chemotherapy. While classical CYP-mediated DDIs do not apply, PK interactions can occur through shared target engagement (competitive binding), FcγR-mediated clearance changes, or immunogenicity modulation. Combination PK must be characterised to support rational dosing.

questions.question: Is the impact of the biologic on vaccines or concomitant immunosuppressive therapy assessed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the biologic's impact on vaccine response (particularly for immune-modulating mAbs) and on the PK/PD of concomitant immunosuppressive agents is assessed. Assessment of whether live vaccine contraindications are addressed in labeling.
questions.rationale: Immune-modulating biologics can impair vaccine responses — a labeling and patient safety concern. Anti-CD20 mAbs, for example, ablate B cells and prevent vaccine seroconversion. This must be characterised and communicated in labeling. This interaction category has no SM parallel.

questions.question: Are the effects of body weight, disease burden, and baseline target levels on PK/PD characterised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether key biologic-specific covariates are assessed: body weight (most important covariate for mAb PK, drives flat vs. weight-based dosing), disease burden/tumour burden (affects TMDD-driven clearance), baseline target levels (soluble and membrane-bound target affecting drug disposition), and albumin (affects FcRn-mediated recycling).
questions.rationale: For biologics, the intrinsic factors that matter most are fundamentally different from SM. Body weight, disease burden (affecting target-mediated clearance), albumin (affecting FcRn recycling), and baseline target levels are the primary PK covariates — not CYP polymorphisms, renal function, or hepatic function. Understanding these covariates drives dosing strategy.

questions.question: Is a dedicated organ impairment study required, or is a waiver justified based on the biologic's elimination mechanism?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether renal and hepatic impairment studies are needed or scientifically waived. For mAbs (eliminated by proteolytic catabolism, not renal/hepatic clearance), dedicated organ impairment studies are generally not required per FDA guidance. Assessment of whether waiver justification is documented and whether disease-related organ impairment could indirectly affect PK through altered target expression or FcRn function.
questions.rationale: mAbs are not renally or hepatically cleared — they are too large for glomerular filtration and are eliminated by intracellular proteolysis. Dedicated renal/hepatic impairment studies are generally not required. However, organ impairment may indirectly affect PK through altered albumin/FcRn, target expression, or distribution. The waiver justification must be documented per FDA guidance.

questions.question: Race/ethnicity: Are there adequate PK data in relevant global populations?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK data are available in diverse populations. For biologics, racial/ethnic differences in target expression, FcγR polymorphisms (affecting ADCC-dependent mAbs), and FcRn polymorphisms may affect PK/PD. Assessment of whether these are explored.
questions.rationale: While CYP polymorphism-driven racial PK differences are not relevant for biologics, FcγR polymorphisms (affecting ADCC-mediated efficacy for some mAbs) and potential differences in target expression across populations may affect PK/PD. Global regulatory agencies expect representative population data.

questions.question: Is the impact of anti-drug antibodies (ADA) on exposure and clinical outcomes characterised as a key intrinsic factor?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether ADA is treated as a primary intrinsic factor in exposure-response analyses, including stratification of efficacy and safety by ADA status, time-course of ADA impact on trough concentrations, and whether ADA-mediated exposure loss correlates with loss of clinical response.
questions.rationale: ADA is the most important "intrinsic factor" for biologics — equivalent in impact to CYP2D6 poor metaboliser status for SM. ADA-positive patients may have dramatically different PK, PD, efficacy, and safety profiles. Treating ADA as a primary covariate (not a secondary analysis) in exposure-response is essential for understanding the true therapeutic profile.

questions.question: Paediatrics: Is there a paediatric PK/dosing plan using appropriate scaling for biologics?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether paediatric PK strategies account for biologic-specific considerations: body weight-based or BSA-based dosing, age-dependent FcRn maturation, developmental changes in target expression, and whether allometric scaling (not simple mg/kg) is used. Assessment of whether PBPK or PopPK modelling supports paediatric dose selection.
questions.rationale: Paediatric dosing of biologics requires consideration of age-dependent FcRn expression (affecting half-life), body composition changes, and potential differences in target biology. Simple mg/kg scaling from adults is often inadequate. Modelling-based approaches are increasingly expected by regulators.

questions.question: Elderly: Are elderly patients included or studied, with assessment of age-related changes in biologic disposition?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether elderly patients are included and age-related PK/PD changes assessed. For biologics, includes assessment of whether age-related changes in FcRn function, immune senescence, albumin levels, and disease characteristics affect PK/PD.
questions.rationale: Age-related changes in immune function (immunosenescence) may affect biologic PD, particularly for immune-engaging biologics. FcRn function and albumin levels may change with age, potentially affecting biologic PK. These effects are distinct from SM age-related clearance changes.

questions.question: Pregnancy/lactation: Is placental transfer via FcRn and breast milk excretion characterised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether IgG-based biologic transfer across the placenta (via FcRn, increasing through trimesters) and excretion into breast milk are characterised or modeled. Assessment of whether fetal/neonatal exposure and potential immune effects are addressed in labeling and risk management.
questions.rationale: IgG mAbs actively cross the placenta via FcRn with increasing efficiency in the second and third trimesters, exposing the fetus directly to the biologic. This is a fundamentally different mechanism from SM passive placental transfer. mAbs are also excreted in breast milk (though likely degraded in infant GI tract). These exposures must be characterised for labeling and risk communication.

questions.question: Is a PopPK model developed incorporating TMDD and biologic-specific covariates?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PopPK models use TMDD-aware structural models (quasi-steady-state, Michaelis-Menten approximation, or full TMDD) and incorporate biologic-relevant covariates (body weight, ADA status, baseline target levels, albumin, disease burden). Assessment of model validation and predictive performance.
questions.rationale: PopPK for biologics requires TMDD-aware model structures — standard compartmental models cannot capture the non-linear PK that defines biologic disposition. Covariate analysis must focus on biologic-relevant factors (weight, ADA, target levels) rather than SM-relevant factors (CYP genotype, renal function). Model structure choice directly impacts dose recommendations.

questions.question: Are covariates (body weight, ADA status, disease burden) incorporated into dosing guidance?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether significant covariates are translated into practical dosing recommendations. For biologics, includes the flat vs. weight-based dosing decision (informed by body weight covariate magnitude), ADA-driven dose adjustments, and disease burden-driven dose individualisation.
questions.rationale: Translating biologic-specific covariate effects into dosing guidance is critical. The flat vs. weight-based dosing decision alone can determine whether a biologic requires multiple dose strengths and weight-band tables or can use a single fixed dose — with major implications for manufacturing, supply chain, and clinical convenience.

questions.question: Are model files available for inspection (e.g., NONMEM, Monolix)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether model files are archived, accessible, and reproducible.
questions.rationale: Transparent model documentation is critical for reproducibility, regulatory review, and audit readiness.

questions.question: Are simulations supporting dose selection and labeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether simulation outputs inform dosing strategies and label recommendations. For biologics, includes simulations of trough concentration distributions, target coverage probability, dose interval optimisation, and exposure in special populations (obese, cachectic, high tumour burden, ADA-positive).
questions.rationale: Simulations for biologics must address biologic-specific scenarios: probability of achieving target trough concentrations, impact of ADA emergence on long-term exposure, dose adjustment scenarios for high/low body weight, and target coverage maintenance at proposed dosing intervals.

questions.question: Is PBPK modelling used to support biologic-specific PK predictions (SC absorption, tissue distribution, FcRn-mediated disposition)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether biologic-specific PBPK models incorporating FcRn-mediated recycling, SC absorption (lymphatic uptake), tissue distribution based on target expression, and TMDD are developed. Assessment of whether platforms designed for large molecules (e.g., PK-Sim, proprietary mAb PBPK) are used rather than SM-focused PBPK tools.
questions.rationale: PBPK for biologics is fundamentally different from SM PBPK — it addresses FcRn recycling (not hepatic metabolism), SC absorption via lymphatic system (not GI absorption), and target-mediated tissue distribution (not partition coefficients). SM-focused PBPK tools (Simcyp, GastroPlus) have limited applicability for large molecules. Dedicated biologic PBPK platforms are required.

questions.question: Are PBPK models built with adequate quality, documentation, and validated against clinical data?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PBPK models are structurally appropriate, well-documented, and validated against observed clinical PK data.
questions.rationale: Model quality and validation against observed data are essential for regulatory credibility.

questions.question: Are PBPK models submitted to regulators to support dose justification or special population predictions?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PBPK models are included in regulatory submissions to support SC dose selection, paediatric dose extrapolation, or organ impairment waiver justification.
questions.rationale: Regulatory submission of PBPK models for biologics is an emerging practice — particularly to support paediatric dose extrapolation, IV-to-SC switching, and organ impairment study waivers. FDA acceptance of biologic PBPK is growing but requires robust validation.

questions.question: Is a Paediatric Study Plan (iPSP / PIP) in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether formal paediatric plans are submitted and agreed with agencies. For biologics, includes consideration of age-dependent FcRn maturation, immune system development, and whether the indication exists in paediatric populations.
questions.rationale: Regulatory authorities require early paediatric planning. For biologics, the plan must address biologic-specific considerations (FcRn maturation, immune development) in addition to standard paediatric requirements.

questions.question: Are regulatory interactions (pre-IND, EOP2, Scientific Advice) documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Documentation of regulatory feedback on clinical pharmacology topics.
questions.rationale: Capturing and responding to regulatory feedback is vital for alignment with agency expectations.

questions.question: Is the clinical pharmacology package adequate to support dose justification in pivotal studies?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the totality of PK, PD, E-R, TMDD, and immunogenicity data justify the pivotal dose(s). For biologics, includes assessment of whether the recommended Phase 2 dose (RP2D) is supported by target coverage, E-R, and safety data — and for bispecifics, whether the RP2D accounts for step-up dosing and CRS mitigation.
questions.rationale: For biologics, dose justification for pivotal studies must integrate TMDD-aware PK, target coverage-based PD, ADA impact, and E-R for both efficacy and immune-mediated safety. This is substantially more complex than SM pivotal dose justification.

questions.question: Is there a clear relationship between exposure and adverse events, including immune-mediated toxicities?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether E-AE relationships are explored. For biologics, includes exposure-CRS relationship (particularly for bispecifics), exposure-infection risk (for immunosuppressive mAbs), exposure-infusion reaction relationship, and ADA-mediated safety events.
questions.rationale: For biologics, the dominant AE categories are immune-mediated (CRS, infections, infusion reactions, autoimmune events) — not organ toxicity driven by peak exposure as in SM. E-AE modeling must address these biologic-specific safety signals and their relationship to exposure, dose intensity, and immune status.

questions.question: Are there modeling-based safety margins for critical toxicities?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether quantitative safety margins are modeled. For biologics, includes CRS risk thresholds (exposure-cytokine relationships), exposure-infection risk margins, and for bispecifics, neurotoxicity (ICANS) exposure thresholds.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety margins are defined differently — not by Cmax-driven organ toxicity but by immune-mediated toxicity thresholds. For bispecifics, CRS and ICANS have identifiable exposure thresholds that can be modeled and used to inform dose capping and mitigation strategies.

questions.question: Are PK/PD/clinical data integrated to support risk–benefit claims?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether data are quantitatively integrated. For biologics, includes integration of ADA data, immunogenicity impact, and for combination regimens (bispecific + checkpoint inhibitor), integrated benefit-risk across the combination.
questions.rationale: Integrated modeling for biologics must incorporate immunogenicity data alongside PK/PD/efficacy/safety — ADA impact on exposure, efficacy loss, and safety is a critical dimension that must be woven into the benefit-risk narrative.

questions.question: Is the current dataset adequate to support biologic-specific labeling claims?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the clinical pharmacology data package supports label content. For biologics, includes immunogenicity section (ADA incidence, impact on PK/efficacy/safety), dosing section (flat vs. weight-based justification, dosing interval), SC administration instructions (if applicable), and interaction section (cytokine-mediated DDI assessment).
questions.rationale: Biologic labeling requires sections that do not exist for SM: immunogenicity (USPI Section 6.2), specific dosing instructions for IV administration or SC self-injection, and interaction information framed around immune-mediated effects rather than CYP DDIs. The clinical pharmacology package must support all of these.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology sections of the IB and draft label consistent?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether IB and labeling present a coherent narrative.
questions.rationale: Inconsistencies create confusion and undermine credibility with regulators and partners.

questions.question: Are gaps expected to be acceptable or flagged by regulatory authorities?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether deficiencies are well-characterised and anticipated. For biologics, common acceptable gaps include organ impairment studies (typically waived), classical DDI studies (not applicable), and food effect studies (not applicable for parenteral products).
questions.rationale: For biologics, many SM clinical pharmacology "requirements" are appropriately waived (renal/hepatic impairment studies, CYP DDI studies, food effect studies, bioequivalence studies). Documenting these waivers with scientific justification is essential — the absence of these studies is expected and acceptable when properly justified, but failure to document the rationale can trigger regulatory questions.

questions.question: Are clinical pharmacology study reports complete and accessible?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether reports are finalised, archived, and retrievable.
questions.rationale: Report completeness and accessibility are essential for submissions and audits.

questions.question: Are datasets (e.g., SDTM, ADaM) and model files well-documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether datasets and models are organised, annotated, and CDISC-compliant.
questions.rationale: High-quality documentation ensures regulatory compliance, reproducibility, and rapid query response.

questions.question: Is there a data room with sufficient documentation to enable investor/regulator review?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a data room is prepared with key clinical pharmacology files.
questions.rationale: A well-prepared data room accelerates external assessments and signals organisational maturity.

questions.question: Is the immunogenicity monitoring strategy integrated into the clinical pharmacology plan (not siloed in bioanalytical)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether immunogenicity assessment is treated as a core clinical pharmacology deliverable — with ADA incidence, titre, neutralising capacity, and clinical impact (PK, PD, efficacy, safety) analysed as part of the integrated clinical pharmacology package. Assessment of whether immunogenicity data are included in exposure-response analyses and PopPK models as a covariate.
questions.rationale: Immunogenicity monitoring is often siloed in bioanalytical groups and disconnected from clinical pharmacology interpretation. However, ADA impact on PK, PD, efficacy, and safety is fundamentally a clinical pharmacology question. Integrating immunogenicity data into PopPK models, E-R analyses, and dose optimisation is essential for understanding the true therapeutic profile of the biologic.

questions.question: Is the clinical immunogenicity risk characterised and integrated into benefit-risk?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the clinical immunogenicity profile is characterised including ADA incidence over time, impact on trough concentrations and steady-state exposure, correlation with efficacy loss or safety events (infusion reactions, hypersensitivity), and whether risk mitigation strategies (dose adjustment, immunosuppressive co-medication, alternative formulation) are supported by data.
questions.rationale: Clinical immunogenicity is the biologic equivalent of CYP pharmacogenomics for SM — it defines a major source of inter-patient variability in exposure and response. Programmes that do not characterise and integrate immunogenicity risk into benefit-risk assessment leave a critical gap in the clinical pharmacology package.

questions.question: Are immunogenicity-related dosing adjustments or mitigation strategies supported by PK/PD data?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether data support specific interventions for ADA-positive patients: dose escalation to overcome clearing ADA, addition of immunosuppressive co-medication (e.g., methotrexate), IV loading before SC maintenance to reduce immunogenicity risk, or switching to alternative biologic. Assessment of whether these strategies are supported by exposure-response data.
questions.rationale: For biologics where immunogenicity significantly impacts clinical outcomes, dosing adjustments or mitigation strategies may be needed. The clinical pharmacology package must provide the E-R evidence base for these interventions — this is an active area of regulatory expectation and clinical innovation.

questions.question: Is a target occupancy or target coverage-based dosing strategy defined?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the dosing rationale is grounded in target occupancy (RO) or target coverage (maintaining trough concentration above the minimum for target saturation). Assessment of whether RO is measured or predicted using PK/PD modeling, and whether the minimum effective trough concentration is defined.
questions.rationale: Target occupancy/coverage-based dosing is the foundational dosing paradigm for biologics — analogous to the MIC-based dosing paradigm for antibiotics. The minimum trough concentration required to maintain target saturation (and therefore pharmacological activity) defines the dose and dosing interval. This concept does not exist for most small molecules and is central to biologic clinical pharmacology.

questions.question: Is the dosing interval justified based on target coverage duration and PK half-life?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the dosing interval is justified by the relationship between PK half-life, trough concentration, and duration of target coverage. Assessment of whether simulations demonstrate that the proposed dosing interval maintains target occupancy above the minimum effective level for the entire dosing interval.
questions.rationale: Biologic dosing interval is determined by how long the drug maintains target coverage — not by convenience-based intervals. A mAb with a 21-day half-life may support Q3W dosing if trough concentrations maintain target saturation, but may require Q2W dosing if the therapeutic target requires higher occupancy. This analysis is unique to biologics.

questions.question: For bispecifics: is the dosing regimen optimised for both efficacy and CRS mitigation (step-up dosing, maintenance dosing)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the bispecific dosing regimen incorporates step-up dosing (low initial doses escalating to target dose to mitigate CRS), intra-patient dose escalation schedule, transition to maintenance dosing, and whether the step-up and maintenance regimen are justified by PK/PD modelling and clinical E-R data.
questions.rationale: Step-up dosing is now standard for T-cell engaging bispecifics — initial low doses allow cytokine release at manageable levels before escalation to the full target dose. The step-up schedule, number of steps, and transition to maintenance are critical clinical pharmacology decisions that distinguish bispecific dose optimisation from conventional mAb dosing. This is a major area of clinical pharmacology innovation.

questions.question: For oncology biologics: is the dose optimisation strategy aligned with FDA Project Optimus and the August 2024 final guidance?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the dose-finding strategy identifies the optimal biological dose (OBD) rather than relying solely on MTD. Assessment of whether the programme incorporates early dose-ranging or randomised dose comparison, whether dose optimisation evidence is generated before pivotal trials, and whether the strategy is discussed with FDA at pre-IND or End-of-Phase 1 meetings. Evaluation of whether the dose optimisation plan addresses FDA's expectation for comparison of multiple dosages.
questions.rationale: FDA's Project Optimus (final guidance August 2024) represents a paradigm shift in oncology dose selection. For biologics and bispecifics, where target saturation often occurs well below the MTD, OBD identification is particularly critical. FDA now expects sponsors to compare multiple dosages either before or within registrational trials. Programmes that proceed to pivotal studies without dose optimisation evidence face increasing regulatory risk, including clinical holds or post-approval dose modification requirements.

questions.question: Is there evidence that the recommended Phase 2 dose (RP2D) represents the optimal biological dose, not just the maximum tolerated dose?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the RP2D is supported by a weight-of-evidence assessment integrating PK/PD data (target coverage, receptor occupancy), exposure-response for efficacy and safety, biomarker evidence of biological effect, and tolerability data. Assessment of whether randomised dose-comparison data or model-based dose selection support the RP2D. For bispecifics, evaluation of whether the step-up and maintenance dose regimen has been optimised across multiple dose levels.
questions.rationale: Post-marketing dose modifications have become common for oncology biologics approved at the MTD rather than the OBD. FDA now expects prospective evidence that the RP2D maximises benefit-risk — not just tolerability. For bispecifics, RP2D selection is particularly complex because efficacy may plateau or decline at higher doses while CRS risk continues to increase. Dose expansion cohorts at a single dose level are no longer considered sufficient evidence of dose optimisation.

questions.question: Are model-informed approaches (Bayesian, BOIN, CRM, QSP) used to support dose-finding and dose optimisation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether model-informed drug development approaches are used to guide dose escalation (Bayesian, BOIN, CRM designs), identify the OBD range, and simulate expected outcomes at different dose levels. Assessment of whether quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) models are used to integrate PK, target engagement, immune activation, and tumour dynamics for bispecifics.
questions.rationale: FDA and EMA increasingly expect model-informed approaches to dose optimisation. For bispecifics, QSP models incorporating trimer formation kinetics, tumour-immune microenvironment dynamics, and cytokine release can predict the OBD range with higher precision than empirical dose escalation alone. Model-informed approaches have demonstrated the ability to increase FIH starting doses by 10-100 fold, saving years of dose escalation.

questions.question: Is tissue distribution and target-site exposure characterised, including tumour penetration for oncology biologics?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether tissue distribution and target-site exposure are assessed beyond systemic PK. For oncology biologics, includes tumour penetration assessment (biopsy PK, imaging PK with radiolabeled antibody), assessment of factors limiting tissue access (molecular size, FcRn-mediated transcytosis, tumour vasculature, antigen sink), and whether tissue PK is incorporated into PK/PD modelling.
questions.rationale: Systemic PK alone may not predict target-site exposure for biologics. mAbs penetrate solid tumours poorly due to size, convective transport limitations, and antigen sink effects. Understanding tumour penetration is critical for dose selection in solid tumour indications. Emerging data from radiolabeled antibody imaging and tumour biopsy PK studies are increasingly informing dose optimisation for oncology biologics.

questions.question: For combination regimens: is the exposure-response for the biologic characterised in the context of co-administered therapies?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether E-R analyses account for the impact of combination partners (checkpoint inhibitors, chemotherapy, other biologics) on the biologic's efficacy and safety profile. Assessment of whether the contribution of each component to combination efficacy is characterised, whether the biologic's dose needs modification in combination, and whether additive or synergistic PD effects are quantified.
questions.rationale: Biologics are increasingly used in combination — bispecifics with checkpoint inhibitors, mAbs with chemotherapy, dual biologics. Understanding each component's contribution to combination E-R is a regulatory expectation (FDA guidance on co-development of two or more new investigational drugs). For bispecifics combined with checkpoint inhibitors, overlapping immune activation may alter the CRS risk profile, requiring combination-specific E-R assessment.

questions.question: Are dosing considerations for obese and cachectic patients addressed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK/PD in patients at body weight extremes is characterised and whether dosing recommendations account for obesity (BMI >30-35) and cachexia. For flat-dosed biologics, assessment of whether fixed doses provide adequate target coverage in heavy patients and avoid excessive exposure in light/cachectic patients. For weight-based dosing, assessment of whether dose capping is implemented for obese patients.
questions.rationale: Body weight is the most important PK covariate for biologics. For flat-dosed mAbs, patients at weight extremes may have significantly different exposures — heavy patients may be undertreated while light patients may be overexposed. For weight-based regimens, dose capping in obese patients is common practice but must be justified by E-R data. Oncology patients with cachexia present the opposite challenge. These edge cases are clinically common and require explicit dosing guidance.

questions.question: Are target-mediated pharmacokinetic interactions between co-administered biologics characterised?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PK interactions driven by shared or overlapping target biology are assessed when two biologics are co-administered. For example, anti-CD20 (depleting B cells) may reduce antigen sink for a second B-cell-targeting biologic; anti-PD-1 may alter T-cell dynamics affecting a T-cell engaging bispecific's PK and PD. Assessment of whether combination PK studies are designed to capture these interactions.
questions.rationale: When two biologics share or modulate the same biological pathways, target-mediated PK interactions can occur — one biologic may alter the target pool available for the other, changing its disposition. These interactions are mechanistically distinct from classical DDIs and cannot be predicted from in vitro studies. They require clinical PK data from the combination to identify and characterise.
